coredata.dc:title: 3D nitrogen-doped carbon nanofoam arrays embedded with PdCu alloy nanoparticles: Assembling on flexible microelectrode for electrochemical detection in cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Taoqun
Wu, Ying
She, Jun
Xu, Yun
Zhang, Yan
Zhao, Anshun
Manoj, Devarajan
Xi, Jiangbo
Sun, Yimin
Ren, Jinghua
Xiao, Fei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a novel and facile strategy for the synthesis of a highly active and stable electrocatalyst based on PdCu alloy nanoparticles (PdCu-ANPs) embedded in 3D nitrogen-doped carbon (NC) nanofoam arrays (NFAs), which were assembled on flexible carbon fiber (CF) microelectrode for in situ sensitive electrochemical detection of biomarker H2O2 in cancer cells. Our results showed that NC-NFAs support possessed a unique hierarchically porous architecture by integrating the macrospores in arrays scaffold within mesopores in individual NC nanofoam, which offered exceptionally large surface area for embedding high-density PdCu-ANPs in it as well as facilitated the mass transfer and molecular diffusion during the electrochemical reaction. Taking the advantages of the unique structural merit of NC-NFAs support and excellent electrocatalyitc properties of PdCu-ANPs that embedded in it, the resultant PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs modified CF microelectrode exhibited good electrochemical sensing performances towards H2O2 including a wide linear range from 2.0 μM to 3.44 mM, a low detection limit of 500 nM, as well as good reproducibility, stability and anti-interference ability. When used in real-time in situ tracking H2O2 secreted from different types of human colorectal cancer cells, i.e., HCT116, HT29, SW48 and LoVo, it can distinguish the types of cancer cells by measuring the number of extracellular H2O2 molecules released per cell, which demonstrates its great promise in cancer diagnose and management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PdCu alloy nanoparticles
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanofoam arrays
Flexible nanohybrid fiber microelectrode
Electrochemical sensing
Cancer cell detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 released from living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF: i) Growth of CuO–NFAs on CF via hydrothermal reaction; ii) Coating of PDA layer on CuO nanofoam by self-polymerization; iii) Decoration of Pd-NPs on PDA layer by chemical reduction; iv) Formation of PdCu-ANPs embedded in NC nanofoam by high-temperature carbonizing and HCl etching; v) Utilization of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF for in situ detection in living cells.

SEM images of (a) bare CF, (b)–(d) CuO–NFAs/CF, and (e)–(h) PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF with different magnifications. (i)–(k) TEM images of PdCu-ANPs/NC with different magnifications. (l) HRTEM image of PdCu-ANP on NC. (m) Scanning elemental mapping of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs nanohybrid. Curve fits of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) Cu 2P and (f) Pd 3d.

CV curves of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF, Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF, NC-NFAs/CF and bare CF microelectrodes in (a) 0.1 M KCl solution containing 1.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- and (b) 0.1 M PBS solution containing 5 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV s- 1. (c) CV curves of PdCu-ANPs/NC- NFAs/CF microelectrode in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0, 1, 2, 5 and 10 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV s- 1. (d) Typical amperometric i-t response of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode to the successive addition of different concentrations of H2O2 into 0.1 M PBS under stirring. Applied potential: −0.06 V. (e) Variation of the relative current response of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode to 200 μM H2O2 in 0.1 M PBS solution with time. Inset is the relative current response of eight different PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrodes to 200 μM H2O2 in 0.1 M PBS solution. (f) Amperometric i-t response of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode to the successive addition of H2O2, GLU, DA and UA in 0.1 M PBS under stirring. Applied potential: −0.06 V.

(a) Amperometric current response of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode to the addition of 1 mg mL−1 fMLP and 500 U mL−1 catalase in the vessel with and without 5.0 × 106 LoVo cells. (b) Amperometric current responses of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode to the addition of 1 mg mL−1 fMLP into test solution containing 5.0 × 106 HCT116 cells, HT29 cells and SW48 cells.

head.ce:title: 3D nitrogen-doped carbon nanofoam arrays embedded with PdCu alloy nanoparticles: Assembling on flexible microelectrode for electrochemical detection in cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Plasma Chemistry and Advanced Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a novel and facile strategy for the synthesis of a highly active and stable electrocatalyst based on PdCu alloy nanoparticles (PdCu-ANPs) embedded in 3D nitrogen-doped carbon (NC) nanofoam arrays (NFAs), which were assembled on flexible carbon fiber (CF) microelectrode for in situ sensitive electrochemical detection of biomarker H2O2 in cancer cells. Our results showed that NC-NFAs support possessed a unique hierarchically porous architecture by integrating the macrospores in arrays scaffold within mesopores in individual NC nanofoam, which offered exceptionally large surface area for embedding high-density PdCu-ANPs in it as well as facilitated the mass transfer and molecular diffusion during the electrochemical reaction. Taking the advantages of the unique structural merit of NC-NFAs support and excellent electrocatalyitc properties of PdCu-ANPs that embedded in it, the resultant PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs modified CF microelectrode exhibited good electrochemical sensing performances towards H2O2 including a wide linear range from 2.0 μM to 3.44 mM, a low detection limit of 500 nM, as well as good reproducibility, stability and anti-interference ability. When used in real-time in situ tracking H2O2 secreted from different types of human colorectal cancer cells, i.e., HCT116, HT29, SW48 and LoVo, it can distinguish the types of cancer cells by measuring the number of extracellular H2O2 molecules released per cell, which demonstrates its great promise in cancer diagnose and management.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid and ultra-sensitive detection of cancer biomarkers has attracted much attention as it plays an important role in the early clinical diagnosis and subsequent treatment of cancer diseases [1–3]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including the oxygen radicals (e.g., O2 ·-, ·OH, RO2· etc.) and some nonradicals (e.g., O3, HOCl, H2O2 etc.) that can be readily transformed into radicals [4], are one type of the typical extracellular signal-regulated species [5] and identified as a major contributor to the damage in biological organisms as well. Among a variety of ROS, H2O2 has been reported to have an important effect on the proliferation, apoptosis and senescence of cells [6–8], and the H2O2 level has relation with the normal physiological activities of cells. Meanwhile, many evidences have showed that the abnormal level of H2O2 has link to the cancerization of tissue [9,10]. Therefore, H2O2 can be applied as a molecular probe in the early-stage identification of cancer cells, and the development of high-performance methods for the highly sensitive and accurate detection of H2O2 appears to be particularly important to the pre-diagnosis and treatment of cancer diseases.

Nowadays, a number of analytical techniques have been employed in detecting H2O2, such as spectrophotometry [11], chromatography [12], fluorescence [13], chemiluminescence [14], and electrochemistry [15–20]. Among them, electrochemical method has several distinct advantages including fast response time, high sensitivity, low detection limit, and low cost [21]. In particular, the recent developed nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor based on the direct electrochemical redox of H2O2 on electrode surface has attracted much attention as it can overcome the limitations of enzyme-based biosensor such as the complex enzyme immobilization procedures, and the poor stability and high cost of natural enzyme [22]. Up to now, various noble metal nanomaterials such as Pt [23–25], Au [26–29], and Pd [30-32] have been developed and applied in nonenzymatic H2O2 electrochemical sensor system for their outstanding electrocatalytic activity and stability. Furthermore, benefited from the unique electronic structures and synergistic effects, bimetal has exhibited much higher catalytic activity, catalytic selectivity, and better resistance to deactivation compared with the monometallic counterparts [33–35]. And several noble metal alloy such as PtAu [36], PtPd [37,38] and AuPd [39] nanomaterials have been reported to demonstrate good performances in electrochemical detection of H2O2. Especially, the utilization of transition metal to form alloys with noble metal can not only reduce the costs, but also cause a negative shift of d-band center position and rearrangement of the surface atoms, resulting in easy desorption of intermediate products and highly active surface for H2O2 electrocatalytic reaction [40,41]. However, the preparation of alloy materials usually requires well-controlled reaction conditions; meanwhile, the alloy nanoparticles are trend to aggregate on the supports, which seriously decrease their catalytic activity. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop facile and effective methods to synthesis highly active and stable alloy electrocatalyst for H2O2 electrochemical sensing systems.

In this work, we developed a new strategy for the synthesis of highly active electrocatalyst based on PdCu alloy nanoparticles (PdCu-ANPs) embedded in 3D hierarchically porous nitrogen-doped carbon (NC) nanofoam arrays (NFAs), which were assembled on flexible carbon fiber (CF) microelectrode for H2O2 electrochemical detection in biological system. As shown in Fig. 1 , for the preparation of the hierarchical nanohybrid electrode, CuO–NFAs were firstly grown on CF by a facile hydrothermal synthesis, which acted as the sacrificed-template for the synthesis of NC-NFAs as well as the Cu source for the further formation of PdCu-ANPs in them. Then dopamine was self-polymerized on the surface of CuO-NFAs to form polydopamine (PDA) layer that tightly coated on CuO–NFAs and served as a reducing agent for the synthesis of Pd nanoparticles (Pd-NPs) from K2PdCl4 precursor. Upon high-temperature carbonizing, PDA layer on CuO-NFAs was transferred into NC layer. Meanwhile, during this process, a certain amount of Cu atoms in CuO species were replaced by carbon atoms and turned into PdCu-ANPs when meeting with Pd-NPs. So far as we know, this method for the preparation of PdCu alloy has never been reported previously. Compared with the previous reported methods for the synthesis of noble metal alloy electrocatalyst, the proposed method is more effective and simple. After removing CuO-NFAs template by acid, NC-NFAs with PdCu-ANPs embedded in them were obtained and wrapped on CF. Our results demonstrate that NC-NFAs support possesses a unique hierarchically porous architecture by integrating the macrospores in array scaffold within mesopores in individual NC nanofoam, which offers exceptionally large surface area for embedding high-density PdCu-ANPs in it as well as facilitates the mass transfer and molecular diffusion through the mesoporous channels for electrochemical reaction. Meanwhile, the doping of nitrogen atoms into carbon species can modify the crystalline structure of carbon and give rise to an enhanced electronic conductivity and wettability, which also to a great extent improve the electrochemical properties of the resultant electrode.

Owing to the synergistic contribution of the unique structural merits of NC-NFAs support and excellent electrocatalyitc properties of PdCu-ANPs that embedded in it, the resultant PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs modified CF (PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF) microelectrode has been used for in situ tracking H2O2 secreted from living cancer cells. Under the optimized conditions, it demonstrates good electrochemical sensing performances towards H2O2, the response rapidly reaches 95% of the steady-state value within 5 s, with a wide linear range from 2.0 μM to 3.44 mM and low detection limit of 500 nM. Furthermore, the nanohybrid microelectrode exhibits good reproducibility, stability and anti-interference ability. When used in the real-time tracking H2O2 released from living human cells by stimulation, the nanohybrid microelectrode can distinguish different types of cancer cells by measuring the number of extracellular H2O2 molecules released per cell, which is of great significance for the pre-diagnosis and treatment to the cancer diseases.

Copper acetate monohydrate, dopamine hydrochloride, tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, potassium tetrachloropalladate (II) and N-formylmethionyl-leucyl- phenyl-alanine (fMLP, ≥99.5%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Urea, hydrochloric acid, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), acetamidophenol (AAP), glucose (GLU), H2O2 (30%), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4) were brought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd (China). All other chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. All solutions were prepared using deionized water (resistivity: 18.25 Ω cm- 1). For electrochemical experiments and cell testing, 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) solution consisting of KH2PO4 and K2HPO4 was used as the electrolyte.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, Germany) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TECNAI G220 UTwin instrument, Netherlands) were employed for the characterization of the microstructure. Chemical-state analysis was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an ESCALAB MKII spectrometer with monochromatic Al-Ka (1486.71 eV), X-ray radiation (XRD, 15 kV and 10 mA) and a hemispherical electron energy analyzer, all peaks corrected by C 1s line at 284.6 eV as standard. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) and chronoamperometric experiments were performed with the CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument Company, Shanghai, China) using a conventional three-electrode system. The working electrode is the flexible fiber microelectrode, the reference electrode is an Ag/AgCl electrode, and the counter electrode is a Pt wire. All potentials were measured vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. The electrochemical tests were carried out in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) at 25 °C.

For the preparation of the nanohybrid microelectrode, the CFs were used as the substrate and sequentially washed by alcohol, acetone, and deionized water. The activation of CFs was performed by soaking CFs in mixed pure H2O2 (30%) and ethyl alcohol (volume ratio 1:1, without any purification) at 60 °C for 48 h. The resultant activated CF was ultrasonically rinsed by deionized water at room temperature for 24 h to remove carbonic acid.

For the growth of CuO-NFAs on CFs, 3.0 g of copper acetate and 1.35 g of urea were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, and a bundle of CFs was then immersed in above solution and stirred for 1 h until the solution become blue and transparent. Then, the CFs were transferred into the autoclave containing the above solution in an electrical oven at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, they were collected and rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and then dried in the oven. After annealing at a temperature of 400 °C for 1 h in a tubular air furnace, the CuO-NFAs/CF was obtained.

For the synthesis of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF, CuO-NFAs/CF was soaked in 100 mL of Tris-buffer solution dissolved with 200 mg of DA. The solution was then stirred twice successively for 12 h and changed once after first 12 h. During this procedure, DA film was self-polymerized on the surface of CuO–NFAs to form PDA layer. The as-obtained CFs were immersed in the K2PdCl4 solution (1.53 mM) and stirred for 1 h in an ice bath, and Pd-NPs were grown on PDA layer. After drying in the oven, they were subsequently placed in a quartz tube furnace and annealed at 700 °C for 1 h within pre-carbonization at 400 °C for 0.5 h. Finally, the sample was placed in 0.5 M HCl (60 °C) for 60 h to remove the CuO–NFAs template, then washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF was obtained. For comparison, Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF was prepared under the same procedure but without immersing the CFs into K2PdCl4 solution, and NC-NFAs/CF was prepared by immersing Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF in 0.5 M FeCl3 solution to remove Cu-NPs.

In this work, four types of human colorectal cancer cells, i.e., HCT116, HT29, SW48 and LoVo, were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). The cells were maintained in a culture medium DMEM containing 10% FBS and penicillin (1%), and grown under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C and subcultured every 3 days. After the growth of ∼80% confluence, the cells were collected by centrifugation and used for electrochemical experiments.

For the preparation of the nanohybrid microelectrode, the flexible CF with a diameter of ∼10 μm has been used as the freestanding electrode substrate (Fig. 2 a). The pristine CF was treated by chemical activation to introduce desired functional groups on its surface and increases its hydrophilicity, which facilitates the growth of close-packed CuO-NFAs on it during hydrothermal process. From Fig. 2b, it can be observed that the deposited CuO-NFAs entirely encompass the CFs to form a typical core-shell structure. The cross-section view shows the nearly directional planes of CuO–NFAs layer to CF surface with a thickness of ∼10 μm (Fig. 2b inset and Supplementary Material, Fig. S1). The higher magnification SEM image reveals that the deposited CuO-NFAs are arranged into well-order array structure on CF substrate (Fig. 2c). The individual CuO nanofoam exhibits a typical thickness less than 1 μm, and there are numerous pores visible in CuO nanofoam (Fig. 2d).

The 3D hierarchically porous PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs nanohybrid was prepared using CuO-NFAs as the template. As shown in Fig. 2e, NC-NFAs on CF exhibit well-defined array structure similar to CuO–NFAs template. However, compared with CuO-NFAs layer on CF, the NC-NFAs layer demonstrates a reduced thickness of ∼500 nm by the calcination and acid etching process (Fig. 2f and g). And the individual NC nanofoam displays a typical cheese-like structure that different from CuO nanofoam. The TEM image collected at the edge of the porous structure reveals that the size of the pores in NC nanofoam is in the range of 5∼50 nm (Fig. 2i and j). As a result, NC-NFAs on CFs possess a unique hierarchically porous architecture by integrating the macrospores in array scaffold within mesopores in individual NC nanofoam, which provide very large surface area and excellent structure stability. Furthermore, the TEM image depicted in Fig. 2k shows that there are numerous nanoparticles that well dispersed on carbon support. From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2l, it can be observed that the average size of the nanoparticles is ∼5 nm, and the ordered lattice fringe with a crystal spacing of 0.307 nm is in good agreement with the (200) spacing planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) PdCu alloy [42]. For comparison, Cu nanoparticle in Cu-NPs/NC-NFA exhibits a crystal spacing of 0.209 nm in accordance with the (111) spacing planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) Cu [43] (Fig. S2). The element distribution in PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF has been shown by SEM mapping, which demonstrates the homogeneously dispersion of C, N, O, Pd and Cu elements (Fig. 2m), confirming the successfully synthesis of PdCu-ANPs on NC-NFAs.

The chemical component and electronic interaction of different elements in PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs nanohybrid were further studied by XPS characterization. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the survey spectrum presents five main peaks assigned to Cu 2p, O 1s, N 1s, Pd 3d and C 1s, respectively. The corresponding fitting curves of these elements are shown in Fig. 3b–f. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s exhibits four kinds of C atoms, i.e., C–C or CC (285.1 eV), C–N (286.7 eV), C–O (288.7 eV), CO (290.6 eV) resulting from NC-NFAs support. The spectrum of N 1s can be deconvoluted into three main peaks corresponding to pyridinic-N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic-N (401.1 eV) and graphitic-N (402.6 eV) (Fig. 3c). These indicate that PDA layer was successfully converted to NC layer after annealing [44]. The O 1s region was fit with two peaks assigned to CO (532.7 eV) and C–O (535.3 eV) (Fig. 3d). Furthermore, the deconvolution of Cu 2p spectrum for PdCu-ANPs displays two main peaks assigning to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 at 932.4 eV and 952.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 3e), which is 0.3 eV negative shift compared with bulk Cu (932.7 eV) [45], and the other two doublet peaks are assigned to Cu (+). The spectrum of Pd 3d exhibits two main peaks of Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2 at 337.5 eV and 342.7 eV, respectively, with two doublet peaks assigned to Pd (+) (Fig. 3f). Similarly, the Pd 3d peaks of PdCu-ANPs display obvious positive shift in comparison with bulk Pd based on reported work [46]. The positive shift of binding energy of Pd 3d demonstrates the electronic and atomic structure change in alloy due to partial electron transfer between Pd and Cu in PdCu alloy [47]. The atomic concertation of different elements is analyzed by the peak area ratio of the XPS spectrum, which is 90.25%, 5.08%, 4.19% 0.37% and 0.12% for C, N, O, Pd and Cu, respectively.

The electrochemical properties of bare CF, NC-NFAs/CF and PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrodes have been investigated using Fe(CN)6 3-/4- as the redox probe. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CV curves of all microelectrodes exhibit distinct symmetric redox peaks assigned to the reversable single electron redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- on electrode surface. However, for bare CF microelectrode, the peak current densities of probe ions are quite low and the peak potential difference (ΔE p) is up to 282 mV. When the CF microelectrode has been wrapped by a layer of NC-NFAs, both the peak current densities and the leveled current separation increase, and ΔE p value decreases to 56 mV. This is because NC-NFAs have good electrical conductivity and characteristic hierarchically porous architecture, which offer effective macro/mesoporous channels for mass transfer and molecular diffusion, and ensure the effective contact of the electrolyte to the electrode surface. While for Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF and PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF, the peak current densities dramatically increase due to the fact that PdCu-ANPs embedded in NC-NFAs provide abundant surface active site and facilitate the electron transfer between electrode and redox species. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of different nanohybrid microelectrodes has been calculated by the Randled-Sevcik equation [48], i p = 269n3/2 AD 1/2 v 1/2 c, where i p is the peak current, n is the number of transfer electron (n = 1), A is the electrode area, D is the diffusion coefficient of the electroactive molecule in solution (D = 6.70 × 10- 6 cm2 s- 1), v is the scan rate of CV test, and c is the concentration of the Fe(CN)6 3-/4- molecules. Accordingly, the ECSA value is 0.134 cm2, 0.333 cm2, 0.567 cm2 and 0.778 cm2 for bare CF, NC-NFAs/CF, Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF and PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrodes, respectively.

Fig. 4b presents the CV curves of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 5 mM H2O2 under the optimized condition, with NC-NFAs/CF and bare CF microelectrodes as the control. The CV curve of bare CF microelectrode exhibits scarcely response toward 5 mM H2O2, while there is a distinct electrochemical reduction peak at −0.4 V for NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode. For comparison, the reduction peak for Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode shifts to −0.15 V and the peak current density increases dramatically. Furthermore, the CV curve of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode demonstrates a remarkable reduction peak at −0.05 V and a weak oxidation peak at +0.35 V. Evidently, the PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF exhibits much higher electrocatalytic activity than Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF due to the synergistic action of Pd and Cu in changing their electronic structure and geometric effect in PdCu alloy electrocatalysts. As the H2O2 concentration increases successively, the reduction peak current densities augment significantly (Fig. 4c). These results systematically demonstrate that PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode possesses extraordinary electrocatalytic activity to H2O2 attributed to the unique structure merits of NC-NFAs support and high electrochemical activity of PdCu-ANPs embedded in them.

The electrochemical sensing performances of bare CF, NC-NFAs/CF, Cu-NPs/NC-NFAs/CF and PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrodes have further been studied by amperometric measurement. Fig. S3 shows that to the successive addition of 0.2 mM of H2O2, PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF exhibits highest amperometric current density among different fiber microelectrodes. Furthermore, upon the successive addition of different concentrations of H2O2, the amperometric response of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode rapidly reaches 95% of the steady-state value within 5 s (Fig. 4d), the detection limit is 500 nM when the signal-to-noise ratio is 3:1, and the current density displays linear range is in the range of 2.0 μM∼0.24 mM and 0.24 mM∼3.44 mM, with a sensitivity of 181.5 and 87.1 μA cm−2 mM−1 (Fig. S4), which is comparable or superior to those of other nanomaterials based nonenzymatic electrochemical H2O2 sensors reported in the past two years (Table S1).

To evaluate the reproducibility of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode for H2O2 detection, eight different electrodes have been tested independently for the amperometric measurement of 200 μM H2O2, which provides a relative standard deviation (RSD) value less than 3.9% (Fig. 4e inset). And the RSD value of reproducible current response is 4.0% with six successive additions of 200 μM H2O2 using one microelectrode. The long-term stability of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode has been investigated by periodically recording its current response to 200 μM H2O2, and the resultant sensor retains 93% of its initial sensitivity at 25 days post-preparation (Fig. 4e). These indicate the good reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the proposed PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode.

The anti-interference ability of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode has been measured by injecting several electroactive species that commonly existed in biological and physiological system, such as DA, UA and GLU into testing solution. Fig. 4f shows that at the applied potential of −0.06 V, the changes of the amperomatric response of 50 μM and 100 μM H2O2 upon adding equal amount of interferent are less than 2%, indicating the high anti-interference ability of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode that resulted from the optimal applied potential close to 0 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 released from living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The practice application of PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode has been explored in sensitive electrochemical detection of H2O2 in living cancer cells, which is of great significance for clinic diagnose. Herein, four types of human colorectal cancer cells, i.e., HCT116, HT29, SW48 and LoVo, were chosen as the target analyte. For in situ real-time tracking H2O2 secreted from these cancer cells, PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF microelectrode was used as the working electrode and carefully placed close to the living cells by the micromanipulators, and fMLP (1 mg mL−1) was used as a stimulator to irritate the cancer cells to secret H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5 a, upon adding 20 μL fMLP in the vessel containing 5.0 × 106 LoVo cells, the amperometric current density dramatically increases by 20 μA cm−2. After adding 20 μL catalase (1 mg mL−1), the scavenger of H2O2 into the solution, the amperometric current density goes down to the background level gradually. However, the control test without cells shows no obvious change in the amperometric signal after the addition of fMLP or catalase. This result reveals that the increased amperometric current density is originated from H2O2 secreted by living cells under the stimulation.

The electrochemical detection of H2O2 released from different types of living human colorectal cancer cells was also performed. Fig. 5b shows that upon the addition of 20 μL fMLP in the test well, the amperometric current densities increase by 3.226 μA cm−2, 2.903 μA cm−2 and 0.968 μA cm−2 for HCT116, HT29 and SW48 cells, respectively. Based on this, the number of extracellular H2O2 molecules released per cell (N o) can be calculated according to the following equation [36]: N o = [(Δj/S) × N A)]/N cell, where Δj is the increase in the amperometric current density of H2O2 released from the living cells under the stimulation, S is the sensitivity of the proposed nanohybrid microelectrode (i.e., 181.5 μA cm−2 mM−1), N A is the Avogadro constant (6.02 × 1023 mol−1), and N cell is the number of cells (5.0 × 106). The calculated N o value for HCT116, HT29 and SW48 cells is 2.14 × 1012, 1.93 × 1012 and 6.42 × 1011, respectively. Evidently, the N o value varies from different types of cancer cells. Therefore, it provides important information to distinguish different types of cancer cells by evaluating their extracellular H2O2 level, which is of great significance in the early clinical diagnose of cancer diseases.

In summary, we have developed a flexible nanohybrid microelectrode based on PdCu-ANPs/NC-NFAs/CF for electrochemical biosensor application. Benefited from the synergistic effect of high electrocatalytic activity of PdCu-ANPs and structural merit of NC-NFAs, the resultant electrode exhibits good electrochemical sensing performances towards H2O2 with respect to high sensitivity, good reproducibility and anti-interference ability, as well as unique flexibility and biocompatibility, which enabled it be used in real-time in situ detection H2O2 secreted by living cancer cells. This provides important information to distinguish different types of cancer cells by evaluating their extracellular H2O2 level, which is of great significance in pre-diagnosis and treatment to the cancer diseases. It can be envisioned that our strategy for designing PdCu-ANPs electrocatalyst on 3D hierarchically porous NC-NFAs scaffold opens new horizon in the development of highly active electrocatalyst and functionalized nanohybrid microelectrode system for electrochemical detection in living cells system, which will contribute to the development of advanced materials, nanotechnology, electrochemistry, biosensing and clinical diagnosis.

Taoqun Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ying Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jun She: Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yun Xu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yan Zhang: Software, Validation, Data curation. Anshun Zhao: Resources, Data curation, Investigation. Devarajan Manoj: Visualization, Data curation. Jiangbo Xi: Formal analysis. Yimin Sun: Resources, Project administration. Jinghua Ren: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fei Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 21874051, 81874084, 51504168 and 51572094), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (Project No. 2019CFB415, 2019CFB539), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Project No. 2019M652616, 2019M652617). We thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology, and the Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics.
